workflow.name: Summarize YouTube videos in Slack using AssemblyAI transcription and OpenAI
workflow.description:
🧠 How it works
This workflow lets users generate structured summaries from YouTube videos directly inside Slack using n8n, AssemblyAI, and OpenAI.

When a user submits a YouTube link via a Slack slash command, the workflow extracts the video ID and validates the video duration. Videos longer than the supported limit are stopped early with a clear message sent back to Slack.

For valid videos, the workflow downloads the video audio as an MP3 file and sends it to AssemblyAI for transcription. Once the transcription is complete, the transcript is passed to an AI model to generate a structured summary.

The final result includes a concise TL;DR, key takeaways, and notable quotes, which are formatted and posted back to Slack asynchronously using the original response URL.

⚙️ Features
	•	Triggers from a Slack slash command with a YouTube link
	•	Validates video length before processing (maximum 10 minutes)
	•	Downloads YouTube audio as MP3 using RapidAPI
	•	Transcribes audio using AssemblyAI
	•	Generates structured summaries (TL;DR, key takeaways, notable quotes)
	•	Posts the summarized result back to Slack asynchronously

💡 Use cases & expected outcomes
	•	Educational YouTube videos → Receive a clear summary instead of watching the full video
	•	Long-form talks or interviews → Quickly get key points and memorable quotes
	•	Research and learning → Extract insights from videos without manual note-taking
	•	Content discovery → Decide whether a video is worth watching based on its summary

In all cases, users receive a clear, structured summary of a YouTube video directly in Slack.

💡 Perfect for
	•	Teams sharing YouTube links and wanting quick context
	•	Researchers and learners reviewing long video content
	•	Content creators analyzing videos efficiently
	

🧩 Notes
	•	Please note that this workflow generates summaries, not full transcripts

options.responseData: Filtering the fluff… give me about 2–4 minutes. I’ll post the summary here. Sifting... 👨‍🍳

values.content:
=You are a professional long-form content summarizer with 25 years of experience. Your job: produce concise, high-signal summaries of video transcripts across any topic. Be neutral, factual, and skimmable for Slack.

Rules
• Extract, don’t infer: Include only facts stated in the transcript. No moralizing, no opinions, no external context.  
• Evidence-first bullets: Start bullets with a *bold* entity/date/metric, then the fact.  
• Specifics over adjectives: Keep names, figures, dates, examples. Prefer numbers to descriptors.  
• Clarity > coverage: Drop details that don’t affect the core message.  
• Language: Respond in the transcript’s language (use the majority language if mixed).  
• Formatting (Slack Markdown only):  
  – Use *bold* and _italics_ (single asterisks/underscores).  
  – Use bullets (•) for lists; one blank line between sections.  
  – No double-asterisk style (**…**), no triple backticks, no code blocks, no emojis (unless provided in transcript).  
  – Keep line lengths skimmable in Slack.  
• Length budgets (hard caps):  
  – TL;DR: ≤ 25 words  
  – Key Takeaways: 5–7 bullets, each ≤ 22 words  
  – Speaker’s Points (only if explicitly enumerated): each ≤ 20 words  
  – Notable Quotes: ≤ 2 short quotes or timestamps (optional)  
• Enumerated Points rule: If (and only if) the speaker explicitly lists points/steps/reasons (e.g., “three points… first/second/third”), include a *Speaker’s Points* section that mirrors the numbering exactly.  
• Gaps: If portions are unclear or missing, end with: _Note: Some portions unclear / missing._

Output format (exactly)

*TL;DR*  
{one sentence, ≤ 25 words}

*Key Takeaways*  
• *[Entity/Date/Metric]* brief fact statement.  
• *[…]* …  
• *[…]* …

*Speaker’s Points* (include only if the speaker explicitly enumerated them)  
1. First: …  
2. Second: …  
3. Third: …

*Notable Quotes / Moments* (optional)  
• “…” (timestamp if stated)  
• “…” (timestamp if stated)

Hard guardrails
Do not add opinions, moral framing, recommendations, “my take,” or external context. Do not speculate about motives or guilt. If a claim is ambiguous or not supported by the transcript, omit it or mark it unclear. Maintain a neutral, factual tone at all times.

parameters.name: text

parameters.content:
## How it works

This workflow lets users submit a YouTube link from Slack and receive a clean, AI-generated summary directly in the same Slack channel.

When a user invokes the Slack command, the workflow extracts the YouTube video URL and checks the video duration using the YouTube Data API. Videos longer than the supported limit are politely rejected with a clear message sent back to Slack.

If the video is within the allowed length, the workflow converts the video into an audio file and sends it to AssemblyAI for transcription. The workflow waits while the transcription is processed and periodically checks its status until the transcript is complete.

Once the transcript is ready, the workflow uses an AI model to generate a concise summary including key takeaways and notable points. The final result is formatted for readability and posted back to Slack using the original response URL.

This approach ensures efficient processing, avoids unnecessary API usage, and provides users with fast, readable insights from YouTube content without leaving Slack.

## Setup steps

1. Create a Slack slash command and configure it to send requests to the Webhook node.
2. Add API credentials for YouTube Data API, RapidAPI, AssemblyAI, and OpenAI.
3. Adjust the maximum allowed video duration if needed.
4. Activate the workflow and test it by submitting a YouTube link from Slack.
Extracts the YouTube video ID and validates video duration before processing.
Converts the video to audio and submits it for transcription.
Waits for transcription completion and retrieves the final transcript.
Generates an AI summary and sends the result back to Slack.
Slack command input and payload normalization.

data.details:
The IF node can be used to implement binary conditional logic in your workflow. You can set up one-to-many conditions to evaluate each item of data being inputted into the node. That data will either evaluate to TRUE or FALSE and route out of the node accordingly.

This node has multiple types of conditions: Bool, String, Number, and Date & Time.
